description: Encodes each character of a command as a hex escape sequence using bash ANSI-C quoting ($'\xNN').  Bash interprets these sequences at parse time, reconstructing the original string.  This defeats simple string-matching detections because the command text never appears in plaintext.  Hex encoding is one of the most common bash obfuscation techniques, often combined with eval or bash -c to execute the decoded result.
native_purpose: ANSI-C quoting in bash allows embedding non-printable or special characters in strings using \xNN hex escape sequences
known_behaviors: Command text replaced by $'\xNN' hex escape sequences
Each character encoded as a two-digit hex value
Resulting string passed to eval, bash -c, or executed directly
Original command invisible to static string matching
No external tools required - pure bash feature
indicators: Pattern: $'\x followed by two hex digits repeated multiple times
Long strings of consecutive \xNN sequences
ANSI-C quoted strings ($'...') containing only hex escapes
eval or bash -c preceding $'\x...' encoded strings

description: Encodes command characters as octal escape sequences within ANSI-C quoting ($'\NNN').  Functionally identical to hex encoding but uses base-8 representation.  Some tools detect hex patterns but miss octal, making this a useful evasion variant.  Bash resolves octal escapes at parse time just like hex escapes.
native_purpose: ANSI-C quoting supports octal escape sequences (\NNN) for embedding characters by their octal ASCII values
known_behaviors: Command text replaced by $'\NNN' octal escape sequences
Each character encoded as a three-digit octal value
Bash resolves escapes at parse time before execution
Often combined with eval or direct execution
May be mixed with hex escapes in the same string
indicators: Pattern: $' followed by backslash and three octal digits repeated
Strings of consecutive \NNN octal sequences inside $'...'
ANSI-C quoted strings containing only octal escapes
eval or bash -c preceding octal-encoded strings

description: Encodes characters using unicode escape sequences in ANSI-C quoting ($'\uNNNN' or $'\UNNNNNNNN').  Bash 4.2+ supports these sequences.  Less commonly detected than hex/octal because many detection tools do not account for unicode escapes in shell contexts.  The decoded output is identical to plaintext commands.
native_purpose: Bash ANSI-C quoting supports \uNNNN (4-digit) and \UNNNNNNNN (8-digit) unicode escape sequences for internationalization
known_behaviors: Command text replaced by $'\uNNNN' unicode escape sequences
Requires bash 4.2+ for \u support
Each ASCII character encoded as \u00NN
Can mix with hex/octal escapes in the same string
Decoded at parse time by bash before execution
indicators: Pattern: $'\u followed by four hex digits
ANSI-C quoted strings with \uNNNN sequences
eval or bash -c preceding unicode-encoded strings
High density of \u escape sequences in a single string

description: Encodes the entire command as a base64 string, then decodes and executes it at runtime using a pipeline like 'echo <b64> | base64 -d | bash'.  This is one of the most common shell obfuscation techniques seen in the wild.  Variations include using printf, here-strings, or openssl for decoding.  The encoded payload is opaque to static analysis without decoding.
native_purpose: base64 encoding is used legitimately for safe transport of binary data through text-only channels (email, URLs, configs)
known_behaviors: Command encoded as base64 string piped to base64 -d
Decoded output piped to bash, sh, or eval for execution
Original command invisible in base64 form
May use echo, printf, or here-strings to feed the pipeline
Some variants use openssl base64 -d instead of base64 -d
indicators: Pipeline pattern: base64 -d piped to bash/sh/eval
Regex: echo\s+['"][A-Za-z0-9+/=]+['"]\s*\|\s*base64\s+-d\s*\|\s*(bash|sh)
Long base64-encoded strings (only [A-Za-z0-9+/=] chars) in pipelines
openssl base64 -d variant in execution pipeline

description: Abuses filesystem glob patterns to construct command paths without spelling them out.  For example, /???/??n/w?o?m? expands to /usr/bin/whoami if the filesystem matches.  This works because bash expands glob patterns before execution.  Highly effective against string-matching detections since the actual binary name never appears in the command.
native_purpose: Shell globbing (*, ?, [...]) is a standard feature for matching filenames by pattern in directory listings and commands
known_behaviors: Command paths replaced by glob patterns with ? and * wildcards
Bash expands globs to matching filesystem paths before execution
Requires the glob to match exactly one file for reliable execution
Binary name never appears in plaintext
Filesystem-dependent: fails if directory structure differs
indicators: Paths composed primarily of ? and * glob characters
Regex: /[?*]{2,}(/[?*a-zA-Z]{2,}){1,} used as command
Executable paths with ? replacing individual characters
Pattern: /???/??n/ matching /usr/bin/ or /usr/sbin/
Glob expressions in command position (first token on the line)

description: Reverses the command string and uses the rev utility (or tac for line-level reversal) to reconstruct it at runtime.  The reversed string is piped through rev and then into bash for execution.  This hides the original command from static analysis since reading the reversed text is non-trivial.  Bashfuscator implements this as a command obfuscator mutator.
native_purpose: The rev command reverses each line of input character by character; tac reverses line order.  Both are standard text manipulation utilities.
known_behaviors: Original command string stored in reverse order
rev utility used to reverse the string back at runtime
Reversed string piped to bash/sh for execution
Original command unreadable without reversal
May combine with echo, printf, or variable storage
indicators: Pipeline containing rev piped to bash/sh
Regex: (echo|printf)\s+['"].*['"]\s*\|\s*rev\s*\|\s*(bash|sh)
Reversed text strings that produce valid commands when reversed
rev command appearing in execution pipeline (not typical usage)
Here-string (<<<) feeding rev in an execution pipeline

description: Inserts backslash characters between letters in command names.  Bash treats a backslash followed by a non-special character as that character itself (backslash is a no-op escape).  The command w\h\o\a\m\i executes identically to whoami.  This is one of the simplest yet most effective obfuscation tricks because it breaks exact-string matching for command names.
native_purpose: Backslash is the bash escape character; when placed before a non-special character it produces that character unchanged
known_behaviors: Backslash characters inserted between letters in command names
Bash ignores backslashes before non-special characters
Command executes identically to the unobfuscated version
Simple string matching for command names fails
Can be applied to arguments and paths as well
indicators: Backslash characters between letters in command/path names
Regex: ([a-zA-Z]\\){2,}[a-zA-Z] in a command context
High density of backslashes within a single token
Known command names with backslashes inserted between characters
Backslashes not preceding standard escape sequences (n, t, etc.)

description: Splits command names using adjacent single-quoted string fragments.  In bash, adjacent quoted strings are automatically concatenated: 'wh''oam''i' becomes whoami.  The empty strings between quotes act as invisible joiners.  This breaks string matching since no single quoted segment contains the full command name.
native_purpose: Bash concatenates adjacent string literals automatically; this is used legitimately to include single quotes within single-quoted strings
known_behaviors: Command names split into adjacent single-quoted fragments
Empty string '' inserted between fragments as invisible joiners
Bash auto-concatenates adjacent quoted strings
Full command name not visible in any single fragment
Works for both commands and arguments
indicators: Adjacent single-quoted string fragments forming command names
Regex: '[a-zA-Z]{1,3}'('[a-zA-Z]{0,3}')+ pattern
Empty string '' appearing between quoted fragments
Multiple single-quote pairs in rapid succession within a token
Command-position tokens with more than 4 single-quote characters

description: Splits commands using adjacent double-quoted fragments.  Similar to single-quote concatenation but uses double quotes: "wh""oam""i" becomes whoami.  Bash concatenates adjacent double-quoted strings.  Double-quote variants allow variable expansion within fragments, adding another layer of complexity for analysts.
native_purpose: Bash concatenates adjacent string literals regardless of quoting style; double-quoted strings allow variable expansion
known_behaviors: Command names split into adjacent double-quoted fragments
Empty string "" inserted between fragments
Bash auto-concatenates adjacent quoted strings
Double quotes allow variable expansion within fragments
Can be mixed with single-quote fragments
indicators: Adjacent double-quoted string fragments forming command names
Regex: "[a-zA-Z]{1,3}"("[a-zA-Z]{0,3}")+ pattern
Empty string "" appearing between quoted fragments
Multiple double-quote pairs in rapid succession within a token
Command-position tokens with more than 4 double-quote characters

description: Uses bash variable case manipulation operators (${var^^} for uppercase, ${var,,} for lowercase) to transform strings at runtime.  The command is stored in a non-obvious case and converted before execution.  Since bash commands are case-sensitive, the original casing must be restored, but the stored form evades detection.
native_purpose: Bash 4.0+ supports ${var^^} (uppercase), ${var,,} (lowercase), ${var^} (capitalize first), and ${var,} (lowercase first) for string case transformation
known_behaviors: Commands stored in wrong case then transformed via ${var^^} or ${var,,}
Variable expansion operators modify case at runtime
Original command not visible in stored form
Requires bash 4.0+ for case manipulation operators
Often combined with eval to execute the result
indicators: Variable expansion with case operators: ${var,,} or ${var^^}
Variables containing command names in wrong case
eval preceding case-manipulated variable expansion
Uppercase or mixed-case strings being lowercased for execution

description: Uses bash indirect variable expansion (${!var}) to resolve command names through multiple levels of variable references.  If x='PATH' then ${!x} expands to the value of $PATH.  Attackers chain this to hide the actual command behind layers of indirection, making static analysis difficult.  Combined with eval, this creates deeply obfuscated execution.
native_purpose: Indirect expansion ${!var} is a bash feature for dereferencing a variable whose name is stored in another variable
known_behaviors: Variable names stored in other variables and dereferenced with ${!var}
Multiple levels of indirection possible
Actual command name hidden behind variable chain
eval or direct execution of the resolved value
Static analysis must trace variable assignments to resolve
indicators: Indirect variable expansion: ${!variable_name}
Chain of variable assignments leading to a command
eval preceding indirect variable expansion
Variables whose values are other variable names

description: Abuses bash brace expansion to construct command strings.  While brace expansion normally generates multiple words, creative use with eval or variable assignment can reconstruct commands from individual characters: eval {w,h,o,a,m,i} or by concatenating brace expansion results.  This technique fragments the command into individual characters.
native_purpose: Brace expansion in bash generates multiple argument strings from a pattern, e.g., file{1,2,3}.txt expands to three filenames
known_behaviors: Individual command characters placed in brace expansion lists
Brace expansion output concatenated or joined to form commands
Often combined with eval, printf, or variable assignment
Each character isolated, breaking string-based detection
May use ranges {a..z} for character generation
indicators: Brace expansion with single characters: {a,b,c,d}
printf %s or tr -d used to join brace expansion output
eval preceding brace expansion constructions
Single-character comma-separated lists inside braces

description: Overrides the Internal Field Separator (IFS) variable to change how bash splits words.  By setting IFS to a custom character, the attacker can embed that character in a command string as a stand-in for spaces.  When bash splits the string on the custom IFS, it reconstructs the original command.  This is a powerful technique for hiding command structure.
native_purpose: IFS (Internal Field Separator) controls word splitting in bash; it defaults to space/tab/newline and is used in loops and read
known_behaviors: IFS variable set to a non-standard character
Command string uses the custom IFS character instead of spaces
Bash word splitting reconstructs the command at runtime
Original command structure hidden behind custom delimiters
IFS often restored or scoped to avoid side effects
indicators: IFS variable being set to a non-default value
Regex: IFS=[^\s;]
Command strings with unusual delimiter characters replacing spaces
IFS assignment immediately followed by command execution
Subshell wrapping to scope IFS changes: (IFS=X;...)

description: Extracts individual characters or substrings from existing shell variables (like $PATH, $HOME, $SHELL) using the ${var:offset:length} syntax.  Characters are picked from known variable values and concatenated to form commands.  This is particularly stealthy because no command names are typed at all -- they are constructed from environment data.
native_purpose: Bash substring extraction ${var:offset:length} returns a portion of a variable's value; used for string manipulation
known_behaviors: Characters extracted from environment variables like $PATH, $HOME
${var:N:1} extracts single characters at specific offsets
Extracted characters concatenated to build command strings
No command names present in the script text
Relies on predictable values in well-known variables
indicators: Variable substring extraction: ${VAR:number:number}
Multiple substring extractions concatenated together
References to well-known variables ($PATH, $HOME, $SHELL, $USER)
Evaluated string assembled from individual character extractions

description: Uses bash process substitution <(command) or >(command) to execute code through a virtual file descriptor.  The command inside the substitution is written to a /dev/fd/N file that bash then reads from.  This adds indirection: the actual command is inside a substitution rather than at the top level, complicating log analysis and command-line auditing.
native_purpose: Process substitution <(cmd) creates a virtual file containing the output of cmd; used legitimately for comparing outputs or feeding programs that expect file arguments
known_behaviors: Commands wrapped in <(...) process substitution
bash or source used to execute the process substitution
Creates /dev/fd/N virtual file descriptors
Actual command hidden inside the substitution
Complicates command-line auditing and logging
indicators: Pattern: bash <(echo ...) or source <(echo ...)
Regex: (bash|source|\.)\s+<\(.*\)
Process substitution <(...) used as script argument to bash
/dev/fd/ references in process trees
curl/wget inside process substitution fed to bash

description: Wraps obfuscated commands inside eval or exec calls.  eval parses and executes its arguments as a bash command, allowing string manipulation to produce the command dynamically.  exec replaces the current process with the command.  Bashfuscator layers multiple eval calls to increase obfuscation depth -- the command must be decoded through each eval layer.
native_purpose: eval evaluates a string as a bash command; exec replaces the current shell process with a command.  Both are standard bash built-ins used in scripts.
known_behaviors: Commands wrapped in one or more eval layers
eval arguments constructed from string operations
Nested eval calls increase obfuscation depth
exec may replace the shell process entirely
String construction (concatenation, expansion) fed to eval
indicators: eval keyword appearing before string construction
Regex: eval\s+(eval\s+)*
Nested eval calls (eval eval eval ...)
exec bash -c with constructed arguments
eval receiving output from base64, printf, or variable expansion

description: Encodes the command as a hex string and decodes at runtime using printf with \xNN format specifiers or xxd -r -p.  Unlike ANSI-C hex quoting, this approach constructs the hex externally and uses printf or xxd to decode.  The hex payload can be stored in a variable, file, or piped directly.
native_purpose: printf interprets \xNN as hex byte values; xxd converts hex dumps back to binary.  Both are standard utilities for binary/hex data handling.
known_behaviors: Commands encoded as continuous hex strings
printf '\xNN...' used to decode hex to text
xxd -r -p used to reverse hex dumps to binary/text
Decoded output piped to bash/sh or eval
Hex payload may be split across variables
indicators: printf with \xNN format specifiers piped to bash/sh
Regex: printf\s+['"]((\\x[0-9a-fA-F]{2})+)['"]\s*\|\s*(bash|sh)
xxd -r -p used in an execution pipeline
Long hex strings (only [0-9a-fA-F]) piped to xxd
eval wrapping printf hex-decoded output

description: XOR-encodes the command bytes with a key, then decodes at runtime using a bash loop or python/perl one-liner.  XOR encoding makes the payload appear as random bytes and is reversible with the same key.  The decoding stub is typically a small loop that XORs each byte and builds the command string.
native_purpose: XOR is a fundamental bitwise operation used in cryptography, checksums, and data transformation.  Bash supports XOR via arithmetic expansion $((x ^ y)).
known_behaviors: Command bytes XORed with a key to produce encoded payload
Decoding loop uses $((byte ^ key)) arithmetic
Decoded bytes assembled into command string
Key embedded in the decoding stub
Encoded payload appears as random or binary data
command_examples: key=42; for b in 93 90 85 75 91 93; do printf "\\x$(printf '%02x' $((b^key)))"; done | bash
for x in 29 31 22 20 28 29; do printf \\$(printf '%03o' $((x^73))); done | bash
indicators: Arithmetic XOR operations: $((value ^ key)) in loops
Arrays of numeric values being XORed and decoded
printf constructing characters from XOR results
Byte arrays with a constant XOR key in decoding loops

description: Compresses the command using gzip, then base64-encodes the compressed data for safe embedding.  At runtime, the payload is base64-decoded, gunzip'd, and piped to bash.  This adds a compression layer on top of encoding, making the payload even more opaque to static analysis.
native_purpose: gzip is a standard compression utility; combined with base64 it allows embedding compressed data in text-safe formats
known_behaviors: Command compressed with gzip then base64-encoded
Decompression pipeline: base64 -d | gunzip | bash
Compressed payload smaller but completely opaque
Gzip magic bytes (1f 8b) present in decoded stream
May use zcat as shorthand for gunzip -c
indicators: Pipeline: base64 -d | gunzip/gzip -d/zcat | bash
Regex: base64\s+-d.*\|\s*(gunzip|gzip\s+-d|zcat)\s*\|\s*(bash|sh)
Base64 strings that decode to gzip magic bytes (1f 8b)
gunzip or zcat in execution pipeline
Compressed payload followed by decompression and bash execution

description: Compresses the command using bzip2, then base64-encodes the result.  At runtime, the base64 payload is decoded, decompressed with bunzip2/bzip2 -d, and piped to bash.  Bzip2 produces different compressed output than gzip, potentially bypassing gzip-specific detection rules.
native_purpose: bzip2 is a standard compression utility that uses the Burrows-Wheeler algorithm; used for file compression
known_behaviors: Command compressed with bzip2 then base64-encoded
Decompression pipeline: base64 -d | bunzip2 | bash
Bzip2 magic bytes (BZ) present in decoded stream
May use bzcat as shorthand for bunzip2 -c
Less commonly detected than gzip variants
indicators: Pipeline: base64 -d | bunzip2/bzip2 -d/bzcat | bash
Regex: base64\s+-d.*\|\s*(bunzip2|bzip2\s+-d|bzcat)\s*\|\s*(bash|sh)
Base64 strings that decode to bzip2 magic bytes (BZ)
bunzip2 or bzcat in execution pipeline
Bzip2 decompression chained to bash execution

description: Uses bash here-strings (<<<) or here-documents (<<EOF) to feed commands to bash/sh/eval instead of using echo or printf.  The command text is embedded inline in the script using heredoc syntax, which can span multiple lines and is less obvious in logs.  Combined with encoding or variable substitution, this adds redirection-based indirection.
native_purpose: Here-strings (<<<) and here-documents (<<EOF) are bash features for passing inline text as standard input to commands
known_behaviors: Commands fed to bash via <<< here-string syntax
Multi-line commands embedded in <<EOF here-documents
Here-doc delimiter may be quoted to prevent expansion
Encoded/obfuscated text decoded within here-doc context
Complicates log analysis due to multi-line nature
indicators: Pattern: bash/sh <<< followed by command text
Regex: (bash|sh|eval)\s+<<<\s+
Here-document (<<EOF) feeding bash/sh with obfuscated content
Quoted here-doc delimiter (<<'EOF') preventing variable expansion
Here-string containing encoded or escaped content

description: Uses the ANSI-C quoting mechanism ($'...') to embed escape sequences in command strings.  This is the general form that encompasses hex, octal, and unicode encoding but can also include special escapes like \n, \t, \a, and \e.  The quoting form itself is a detection signal: $'...' strings in command position are unusual in normal scripts.
native_purpose: ANSI-C quoting ($'...') provides C-like escape sequence support in bash for embedding special characters in strings
known_behaviors: Commands wrapped in $'...' quoting with escape sequences
Supports \x, \0, \u, \n, \t and other C escape sequences
Bash resolves all escapes at parse time
Unusual in normal scripts -- strong obfuscation signal
May combine multiple escape types in a single string
indicators: Pattern: $' followed by escape sequences
ANSI-C quoted strings ($'...') in command position
Mixed escape types within a single $'...' string
$' quoting containing primarily escape sequences rather than text

description: Nests commands within $(...) or backtick substitutions to add layers of indirection.  The outer shell evaluates the innermost substitution first, working outward.  Combined with encoding techniques, this creates deeply nested execution where each layer decodes or transforms the next.  Analysts must trace through multiple substitution levels.
native_purpose: Command substitution $(...) captures the output of a command for use as part of another command.  It is a core bash feature used extensively in scripts.
known_behaviors: Multiple levels of $(...) nesting
Each substitution layer may decode, transform, or construct
Innermost commands execute first, working outward
Output of inner commands used as input for outer commands
May combine with eval for execution of final result
indicators: Multiple nested $(...) command substitutions
Regex: \$\(.*\$\(.*\).*\) (nested substitutions)
eval wrapping command substitution chains
Command substitution containing encoding/decoding operations
Deep nesting (3+ levels) of $() substitutions

description: Abuses bash history expansion (!, !!, !-N, !string) to reference and re-execute previous commands.  In interactive shells, !! repeats the last command and !-N references N commands back.  Attackers can stage a command in history and re-execute it via history references, hiding the actual command from the current line being analyzed.
native_purpose: History expansion is a bash interactive feature for quickly repeating or modifying previous commands using ! syntax
known_behaviors: !! expands to the last executed command
!-N references N commands back in history
!string runs the most recent command starting with string
Command being executed not visible on current line
Only works in interactive shells with history enabled
indicators: !! or !-N appearing in command context
Regex: !(!|-[0-9]+|[a-zA-Z]+) in command position
History expansion characters (!) in scripts (unusual)
Sequential commands where second uses history reference
!! following sudo for privilege escalation of previous command
